findings.title: Pin and verify the downloaded installer
findings.whyItBreaks: This executes whatever bytes example.com serves at build time as root, with no version pin or integrity verification. A compromised, changed, or intercepted endpoint can inject arbitrary code into the produced image.

findings.title: Pin and verify the remote installer before executing it
findings.whyItBreaks: The newly added build step downloads bytes from a mutable URL and pipes them directly to `sh`, so a changed or compromised response can execute arbitrary commands during image builds. No pinned artifact or integrity check detects tampering.

findings.title: Pin and verify the downloaded installer
findings.whyItBreaks: Dockerfile:3 downloads and executes content from a mutable URL during every build. If that origin or its served content is compromised or changed, arbitrary code runs in the build and can be embedded in the resulting image.

findings.title: Propagate installer download failures
findings.whyItBreaks: Dockerfile:3 pipelines curl into sh without pipefail or curl --fail. A curl failure with no script input can leave sh to exit successfully, so the build may succeed without installing the required dependency.

findings.title: Preserve missing-key errors in LoadOrDefault
findings.whyItBreaks: Load returns ErrMissing for an absent key, but this exported API ignores it and returns "", exactly the value a present key may legitimately hold. Callers therefore cannot distinguish failed lookup from valid data.

findings.title: Preserve missing-key failures in the exported helper
findings.whyItBreaks: LoadOrDefault discards ErrMissing and returns "", making a missing key indistinguishable from a present key whose legitimate value is empty. As an exported API, callers cannot distinguish failure from data.

findings.title: Preserve missing-key failure in the exported helper
findings.whyItBreaks: The new exported function discards Load's ErrMissing and returns "", making an absent key indistinguishable from a present key with an empty value. Callers cannot handle the missing-key condition.

findings.title: Wait for result senders before returning
findings.whyItBreaks: The newly spawned goroutines can still execute `results <- j * 2` after Run returns. A caller that closes the results channel after Run returns can therefore trigger `panic: send on closed channel`; callers can also observe Run completion before results are available.

findings.title: Wait for result sends before returning from Run
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed goroutine launch makes Run return before its sends complete. A caller that closes its results channel after Run returns can race those goroutines, causing lost results or a "send on closed channel" panic.

findings.title: Wait for result sends before returning
findings.whyItBreaks: Each send now runs after Run may have returned. A caller that closes results after Run returns can cause a goroutine to panic with "send on closed channel"; a process may also exit before pending results are sent. Previously, Run returned only after every send completed.

findings.title: Require admin privileges before allowing record deletion
findings.whyItBreaks: A session with isAdmin=false and a matching tenantId now receives true, bypassing the prior admin requirement for deletion.

findings.title: Require admin privileges before deleting a record
findings.whyItBreaks: Replacing && with || makes canDeleteRecord return true for a non-admin whenever their tenant matches the record. This bypasses the prior administrator requirement and permits unauthorized same-tenant record deletion.

findings.title: Require admin status and tenant match for deletion
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed `||` predicate returns true for a non-admin session in the record's tenant and for an admin session from another tenant. Either case bypasses one of the previously required authorization boundaries.

findings.title: Preserve parse failures instead of saving default settings
findings.whyItBreaks: The new catch converts malformed or schema-invalid persisted settings into defaults. updateFontSize then serializes and saves that replacement, silently discarding recoverable settings such as the user's theme instead of reporting the corruption.

findings.title: Preserve parse failures instead of silently defaulting settings
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed catch at lines 14-15 converts malformed JSON and invalid fields into DEFAULTS. updateFontSize then changes that fallback and calls save, overwriting the invalid persisted value instead of reporting the parse failure.

findings.title: Propagate invalid-settings errors
findings.whyItBreaks: The new empty catch converts malformed JSON and schema-invalid settings into defaults. `updateFontSize` then serializes and saves those defaults with the requested size, overwriting the persisted invalid value instead of preserving the prior observable parse/validation failure.

findings.title: Use the requested branch for report artifacts
findings.whyItBreaks: buildReport records build.ref(branch) as builtFrom but, in the changed return path, fetches artifacts using build.ref("verified"). For any branch other than verified, the returned BuildReport combines the requested branch's commit with artifacts from a different ref.

findings.title: Use the selected branch commit for artifacts
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed return fetches artifacts from `build.ref("verified")`, while `builtFrom` remains the requested branch's commit. A report for `main` can therefore claim `builtFrom` is main while returning verified's artifacts.

findings.title: Keep report artifacts aligned with builtFrom
findings.whyItBreaks: When `build.ref(branch)` differs from `build.ref("verified")`, the returned report sets `builtFrom` to the branch commit but retrieves `artifacts` from the verified commit. Consumers can therefore associate the reported artifacts with the wrong revision.

findings.title: Restore the limit before returning entries
findings.whyItBreaks: For any positive limit smaller than all.length, this returns every reversed entry instead of at most limit. renderFeed calls it with 20 at src/feed.ts:9, so an input with 21 entries makes the loop write index 20 and expands the intended 20-element output to 21 entries.

findings.title: Restore the limit cap
findings.whyItBreaks: For any positive limit smaller than all.length, this returns every reversed entry instead of at most limit. renderFeed passes 20, then writes every returned entry into its fixed-size output array, so feeds with more than 20 entries produce an output longer than 20.

findings.title: Restore the limit when returning recent entries
findings.whyItBreaks: For any positive limit, this returns all reversed entries instead of at most limit. renderFeed passes 20 at src/feed.ts:8, then writes every returned entry at src/feed.ts:10; assigning past index 19 extends the output array.

findings.title: Count only emitted entries in the manifest header
findings.whyItBreaks: The new filter at line 20 removes stale entries from rendered rows, while the header still uses entries.length. For any stale entry, `# entry-count` exceeds the number of emitted data rows.

findings.title: Count only emitted manifest rows
findings.whyItBreaks: Stale entries are removed from `rows`, but the header still receives `entries.length`. A manifest with stale entries declares more rows than it emits.

findings.title: Count only emitted manifest rows
findings.whyItBreaks: The new stale-entry filter omits stale entries from serialized rows, but the header still receives entries.length. The emitted manifest can therefore declare an entry count larger than its actual row count.

findings.title: Preserve the exported legacy formatter
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing exported FormatLegacyID is a source-incompatible API change. Any downstream package using ids.FormatLegacyID will fail to compile on upgrade, with no replacement or deprecation shim provided.

score.error: critic produced no summary or checked list (likely malformed output)
critic produced no summary or checked list (likely malformed output)

findings.title: Return false for incompatible stored-hash values
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed `hmac.compare_digest` call raises TypeError when `stored_hash` is bytes or a non-ASCII string, while the previous equality comparison returned False. `guard` therefore cannot return its normal 401 rejection for such values.

score.error: critic produced no summary or checked list (likely malformed output)

findings.title: Restore execute's string result
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed implementation unconditionally throws, so callers of the exported function can never receive its declared string result.

findings.title: Restore execute's string-returning behavior
findings.whyItBreaks: Every call to the exported execute(input) now throws "always fails" instead of returning the transformed string, making the function unusable on all inputs.

findings.title: Restore execute's result instead of always throwing
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported execute(input) function now throws for every input, eliminating its prior successful behavior of returning input.toUpperCase().

findings.title: Allow active viewers to reach read-only handling
findings.whyItBreaks: For an active viewer below the attempt limit, isEligible now returns false and handle returns "rejected" at line 24. The subsequent viewer-specific "read-only" result at line 25 was previously reachable, so this gate incorrectly blocks a legitimately supported action.

findings.title: Keep viewers on the read-only path
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed viewer check makes an active viewer below the attempt limit fail isEligible; handle at src/handler.ts:24 therefore returns "rejected" instead of reaching its explicit "read-only" branch.

findings.title: Preserve read-only handling for active viewers
findings.whyItBreaks: An active viewer with fewer than five attempts previously passed isEligible and handle returned "read-only" at src/handler.ts:25. The new role check rejects that same request at src/handler.ts:18, making the established viewer behavior unreachable.

findings.title: Keep draft saving independent of payment
findings.whyItBreaks: For an unpaid draft, `canProceed` now returns false. `saveDraft` uses this shared predicate and returns "blocked", regressing the previously allowed ability to save a draft before payment.

findings.title: Do not require payment when saving a draft
findings.whyItBreaks: canProceed is shared by submitOrder and saveDraft. Adding payment === "paid" makes saveDraft return "blocked" for a draft with payment "missing", even though saving an unpaid draft is independent of submission/payment approval.

findings.title: Do not require payment to save a draft
findings.whyItBreaks: For `{ state: "draft", payment: "missing" }`, the new predicate returns false, so `saveDraft` returns "blocked". Before this change the same draft was saved; payment is not a prerequisite for persisting a draft.

findings.title: Keep snapshot files aligned with its commit
findings.whyItBreaks: `commit` remains the requested channel's head, but `files` is read from `stable`. Consumers restoring, comparing, or displaying this Snapshot will attribute stable's file set to the channel commit.

findings.title: Read files from the requested channel head
findings.whyItBreaks: When a channel head differs from "stable", Snapshot.commit identifies the requested channel head but Snapshot.files contains stable's contents. Consumers cannot retrieve files for the commit the snapshot reports.

findings.title: Read files from the selected channel commit
findings.whyItBreaks: For any channel whose head differs from `stable`, `commit` reports `repo.headCommit(channel)` while `files` are read from `repo.headCommit("stable")`. The returned Snapshot therefore misrepresents the contents of its reported commit.

findings.title: Honor the limit parameter
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed public signature accepts `limit`, but the function still uses `max_count = 10`; `build("x", limit=2)` returns ten values rather than two.

findings.title: Honor the public limit parameter
findings.whyItBreaks: build(prefix, limit=3) still returns 10 entries because the implementation uses the fixed max_count = 10. Callers cannot request a bounded result despite the new public parameter.

findings.title: Honor the limit parameter
findings.whyItBreaks: `limit` promises callers control over the result count, but the body still iterates over the hard-coded `max_count = 10`; for example, `build("item", 3)` returns 10 items.

findings.title: Validate that parsed amounts are positive
findings.whyItBreaks: parse_positive_int(-1) returns -1, so charge(-1) returns -100. The exported name promises positive values but permits non-positive amounts.

findings.title: Enforce positivity in parse_positive_int
findings.whyItBreaks: The renamed API newly promises a positive result, but `parse_positive_int("-2")` returns `-2`; its in-file consumer `charge` then returns `-200` at src/validators.py:6.

findings.title: Reject non-positive values
findings.whyItBreaks: `parse_positive_int('-1')` returns `-1`, so `charge('-1')` produces `-100` rather than rejecting an input its public name promises is positive.

findings.title: Preserve the caller's DataFrame
findings.whyItBreaks: The removed defensive copy means assignments now modify df itself and the function returns that same object. Callers that retain or reuse the input DataFrame will observe unexpected value changes.

findings.title: Preserve the caller's DataFrame
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed assignments write directly to df and return that same object, whereas the prior implementation modified a copy. Reusing the input after transformation now exposes unexpected value changes.

findings.title: Preserve caller DataFrame ownership
findings.whyItBreaks: Previously, apply_values returned a modified copy and left df unchanged. It now writes through df.loc, so callers that retain or reuse the input DataFrame observe unexpected value changes.

findings.title: Reject non-positive input or retain the generic parser name
findings.whyItBreaks: `parse_positive_int("-1")` returns `-1`, and `charge("-1")` returns `-100`. The new public name promises a positive result while the implementation performs only an unconstrained `i64` parse.

findings.title: Enforce the positivity promised by parse_positive_int
findings.whyItBreaks: The new public name promises positive results, but the changed function body parses and returns negative integers unchanged. Thus parse_positive_int("-1") returns -1 and charge("-1") returns -100.

findings.title: Enforce positivity promised by parse_positive_int
findings.whyItBreaks: `parse_positive_int` returns any parseable `i64`, including `0` and negatives. The changed caller `charge` therefore still emits negative charges for inputs such as `"-1"`, despite the newly public positive-value contract.

findings.title: Borrow items in the debug macro
findings.whyItBreaks: `dbg!(items)` takes ownership of the `Vec<String>`, then line 3 tries to borrow the moved value for `items.len()`, producing Rust error E0382.

findings.title: Remove the consuming debug call
findings.whyItBreaks: `dbg!(items)` evaluates and returns the owned `Vec<String>`; because its return value is discarded, `items` is moved and `items.len()` on line 3 is a use-after-move compiler error.

findings.title: Borrow items when logging
findings.whyItBreaks: `dbg!(items)` evaluates and returns `items` by value, moving the `Vec<String>`. `items.len()` on line 3 then uses a moved value, producing Rust error E0382.

findings.title: Compute paths against the returned revision
findings.whyItBreaks: The public Changeset contract says changedPaths are computed against fromRevision, but fromRevision is the pinned tag while readChangedPaths receives the separately resolved latest revision. Consumers replaying these paths on fromRevision can receive paths for a different base revision.

findings.title: Compute changed paths from the pinned revision
findings.whyItBreaks: `fromRevision` is the resolved `pinnedTag`, but `changedPaths` is calculated from `latest`. The documented contract says consumers replay paths on `fromRevision`, so differing revisions yield paths for an incompatible base.

findings.title: Compute changed paths from the returned revision
findings.whyItBreaks: `fromRevision` is resolved from `pinnedTag`, while `changedPaths` is calculated from `latest`. When they differ, the returned paths do not describe changes relative to the revision that consumers are instructed to replay on.

findings.title: Update queries to use contact_email
findings.whyItBreaks: After this migration runs, users.email no longer exists, but src/query.sql still executes SELECT email FROM users. Any consumer executing that query after upgrade receives a missing-column SQL error.

findings.title: Update the query for the renamed email column
findings.whyItBreaks: After this migration, users.email no longer exists, but src/query.sql still executes SELECT email FROM users. Any database upgraded through 0002 will fail that query with a missing-column error.

findings.title: Update queries to use the renamed column
findings.whyItBreaks: After this migration, users.email no longer exists, but src/query.sql still executes SELECT email FROM users. Any deployment that applies the migration will fail this query with an unknown-column error.

findings.title: Restore runtime-provided billing credentials
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed line returns the same source-controlled key for every deployment and ignores BILLING_API_KEY. Deployments can no longer select or rotate their own billing credential, including when the environment variable is unset.

findings.title: Restore environment-backed billing credentials
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed line embeds a `bk_live_` billing key in repository source, making it available to anyone with source or build-artifact access. It also makes `makeClient()` ignore `BILLING_API_KEY` for every deployment.

findings.title: Remove the committed live billing key
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed line embeds a `bk_live_` key in source and replaces the prior missing-`BILLING_API_KEY` error path. Calls to this exported function now silently receive that fixed key when deployment configuration is absent.

findings.title: Restore the admin authorization check for purging
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed branches return "forbidden" for `user.isAdmin=true`, but allow `user.isAdmin=false` with an archived project to reach `db.delete(project.id)`. This reverses the prior authorization rule and grants non-admin users destructive deletion.

findings.title: Restore admin authorization before purging
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed guard returns "forbidden" for every admin, then permits any non-admin with an archived project to reach `db.delete(project.id)`. This reverses the prior authorization rule and enables unauthorized permanent deletion.

findings.title: Require an administrator before purging
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed guard returns "forbidden" when user.isAdmin is true. A non-admin with an archived project passes both guards and reaches db.delete at src/projects.ts:14, allowing an unauthorized destructive action.

findings.title: Restore defaults when the settings file is absent
findings.whyItBreaks: `load_settings` returns `None` for a nonexistent path, so `startup` evaluates `None["workers"]` and raises `TypeError`. Before this change, the same path started with one worker.

findings.title: Preserve a valid default when the settings file is absent
findings.whyItBreaks: `load_settings` returns `None` when `path` does not exist, and the changed `startup` body immediately subscripts it. This changes the prior missing-file behavior from returning the one-worker default to raising `TypeError` during startup.

findings.title: Restore the missing-file fallback in startup
findings.whyItBreaks: load_settings returns None when the path does not exist (app/config.py:6-7). This change removes the fallback, so settings["workers"] raises TypeError instead of returning the prior default worker count.

findings.title: Propagate gateway submission failures
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported Charge function now logs a Gateway.Submit error at the changed line and then returns nil, so callers cannot distinguish a failed charge from a successful one.

findings.title: Propagate gateway submission failures
findings.whyItBreaks: When Gateway.Submit returns an error, exported Charge logs it and returns nil, conflating a failed payment with a successful charge for callers.

findings.title: Propagate gateway submission failures
findings.whyItBreaks: When Gateway.Submit fails, Charge logs the failure and returns nil. This exported API now makes a failed charge indistinguishable from a successful one, allowing callers to treat an uncharged payment as complete.

findings.title: Keep tenant ID in the settings cache key
findings.whyItBreaks: All tenants requesting the same setting name now share `settings:${name}`. After tenant A caches `theme`, tenant B's `settingsFor('B', 'theme', store)` returns tenant A's value without calling `store.fetch`, violating tenant isolation.

findings.title: Preserve the tenant-scoped invalidate API
findings.whyItBreaks: This exported function changed from `invalidate(tenantId, name)` to `invalidate(name)`. Existing TypeScript consumers fail to compile; JavaScript consumers passing both arguments silently delete `settings:<tenantId>` instead of the requested setting, leaving stale values cached.

findings.title: Keep tenant ID in the settings cache key
findings.whyItBreaks: The process-global cache key at line 12 omits `tenantId`. A value fetched for one tenant at line 15 is stored under `settings:<name>` and is returned to another tenant with that name from the cache hit at lines 13-14, without calling its store.

findings.title: Preserve the exported invalidate API's tenant parameter
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported function changed from `invalidate(tenantId, name)` to `invalidate(name)`. Existing JavaScript callers passing two arguments will treat the tenant ID as `name` and delete `settings:<tenantId>` rather than the intended cached setting; TypeScript consumers will no longer type-check.

findings.title: Scope cached settings by tenant
findings.whyItBreaks: The cache key now depends only on name, while store.fetch remains tenant-scoped. After tenant A reads a setting named "theme", tenant B reading the same name receives tenant A's cached value without calling its store.

findings.title: Preserve the tenant-scoped invalidate API
findings.whyItBreaks: This exported function previously required (tenantId, name). Existing JavaScript callers passing those two arguments now use tenantId as name, so they delete the wrong cache entry; TypeScript callers fail to compile after upgrading.

findings.title: Serialize concurrent reservations before persisting
findings.whyItBreaks: Two concurrent reserve("sku") calls can both read remaining=1 before either await completes. Each saves 0, sets local stock to 0, and returns true, so two reservations succeed for one unit.

findings.title: Serialize reservations before awaiting persistence
findings.whyItBreaks: Two overlapping calls can both read the same positive `remaining` value, both await `save(sku, remaining - 1)`, then both return true and set the same decremented count. This records and grants two reservations while reducing stock by only one.

findings.title: Make reserve atomic across concurrent calls
findings.whyItBreaks: Two concurrent reserve("sku") calls can both read remaining=1 before either await completes. Both save 0, set local stock to 0, and return true, so one unit is successfully reserved twice.

findings.title: Keep retry delays in milliseconds
findings.whyItBreaks: backoff() now returns milliseconds at src/config/backoff.ts:2, but retry() still calls sleep(backoff(a) * 1000) at src/jobs/retry.ts:12. The first retry waits 1,000,000 ms (16m40s) rather than 1,000 ms; capped retries wait 30,000,000 ms rather than 30,000 ms.

findings.title: Keep retry delays in milliseconds
findings.whyItBreaks: backoff() now returns milliseconds, but retry() still calls sleep(backoff(a) * 1000). The first retry waits 1,000,000 ms (~16.7 minutes) instead of 1,000 ms; capped retries wait 30,000,000 ms (~8.3 hours).

findings.title: Update retry to consume millisecond backoff
findings.whyItBreaks: backoff(0) now returns 1,000 milliseconds, but src/jobs/retry.ts:12 still multiplies it by 1,000 before passing it to sleep(). The first retry waits 1,000 seconds instead of 1 second; capped retries wait 30,000 seconds instead of 30 seconds.

findings.title: Propagate the file close error
findings.whyItBreaks: The new `defer f.Close()` discards its error. Previously, a successful Flush returned `f.Close()`'s error, so callers could detect a final filesystem write/close failure; now Save returns nil after a successful flush even when closing fails.

findings.title: Propagate the file-close error
findings.whyItBreaks: The new deferred close ignores its error, whereas the previous implementation returned f.Close() after a successful flush. Close can report delayed write failures, so exported Save can now return nil despite the file not being durably written.

findings.title: Propagate the file close error
findings.whyItBreaks: The new `defer f.Close()` ignores its result. Before this change, a successful flush returned `f.Close()` directly, so a close-time write failure reached Save callers; now Save returns nil whenever `w.Flush()` succeeds.

findings.title: Iterate through the page end bound
findings.whyItBreaks: `_window_bounds` returns a half-open end, but `range(start, end - 1)` excludes the final valid position. A one-row page becomes empty; every full page loses its last row, so `summarize` omits rows from its output.

findings.title: Preserve legacy keyword argument names
findings.whyItBreaks: All non-private functions renamed parameters (`n`→`value`, `size`→`page_size`, `page`→`page_index`, etc.). In Python, parameter names are part of the callable interface: existing calls such as `page_count(total=10, size=5)` or `render_page(rows=rows, page=0, size=10)` now raise `TypeError`.

findings.title: Include the page end index when collecting rows
findings.whyItBreaks: The half-open bounds helper returns end as the first index after the page, but range(start, end - 1) excludes the final in-page row. A one-row page returns no rows, and every larger page omits its final row; summarize consequently loses one row per page.

findings.title: Include the page end row
findings.whyItBreaks: The bounds are half-open, but range(start, end - 1) excludes end - 1. For example, page_window(['a', 'b'], 0, 2) returns only ['a']; a one-row page returns []. render_page and summarize therefore silently omit the last row of every page.

findings.title: Return entries only while their TTL is valid
findings.whyItBreaks: A newly stored entry has age 0, so with any positive TTL `0 > ttlMs` is false and get() deletes it and returns null. Conversely, entries older than the TTL are returned instead of expired.

findings.title: Remove every completed job when pruning
findings.whyItBreaks: Splicing during forEach shifts the next job into the current index, but forEach then advances past it. For `[done, done, pending]`, the second completed job remains in the returned queue.

findings.title: Return entries only before their TTL expires
findings.whyItBreaks: A newly stored entry has age 0, so it fails `0 > ttlMs`, is deleted, and `get` returns null. Conversely, expired entries are returned indefinitely.

findings.title: Do not splice the array while iterating completed jobs
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing an item shifts the next job into the current index, then `forEach` advances to the following index. Consecutive completed jobs therefore leave every second completed job in the returned queue.

findings.title: Return entries only before their TTL expires
findings.whyItBreaks: The comparison is inverted: a newly stored entry has age near zero and is deleted as a cache miss, while an entry older than ttlMs is returned indefinitely.

findings.title: Filter all completed jobs without skipping adjacent entries
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing an item during forEach shifts the next item left while forEach advances its index. For `[done, done]`, the first job is removed and the second is skipped, so pruneDone returns a completed job.

findings.title: Sort scores numerically before reversing
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed `scores.sort()` compares numeric scores as strings. With scores [2, 10, 9], the reversed result is [9, 2, 10], so `topPlayers(..., 2)` sets a cutoff of 2 and can include the 2-point player while excluding the 9-point player.

findings.title: Sort scores with a numeric comparator
findings.whyItBreaks: Array.sort() compares numbers as strings. With scores 9 and 10, the reversed order is [9, 10]; requesting two players sets cutoff to 10 and drops the player with score 9. Requesting one player can return a 9-point player before a later 10-point player.

findings.title: Restore numeric score sorting
findings.whyItBreaks: Array.sort() without a comparator orders numbers as strings. With scores 20 and 100 and n=1, rankScores produces [20, 100], so topPlayers selects the 20-score player.

findings.title: Restore constant-time webhook signature comparison
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed line compares an attacker-controlled signature with `==` rather than using a constant-time comparison. `handle` uses this boolean to authorize `processor(payload)`, so comparison timing can expose information about the expected HMAC. Use `hmac.compare_digest` for this authentication decision.

findings.title: Restore constant-time webhook signature comparison
findings.whyItBreaks: Using string equality can stop at the first mismatched character, exposing how much of the expected HMAC matches through response timing. The prior hmac.compare_digest call was specifically the constant-time primitive for this authentication boundary; an attacker can use the timing signal to iteratively forge a signature and reach the processor.

findings.title: Restore constant-time HMAC comparison
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed `==` comparison may return after the first differing character, exposing signature-prefix timing differences to remote callers. A valid signature gates `processor(payload)` in the same file.

findings.title: Keep balance updates in the ledger transaction
findings.whyItBreaks: The transaction exits after the ledger insert at line 23. If either balance update then fails, the ledger transfer is already committed without matching balances; if the destination update fails after the source update, funds can be debited without being credited.

findings.title: Keep ledger and balance updates in one transaction
findings.whyItBreaks: The transaction scope ends after recording the ledger entry. If either subsequent balance update fails, the ledger entry has already committed; if the second update fails, the source may remain debited without crediting the destination.

findings.title: Keep ledger and balance updates in one transaction
findings.whyItBreaks: The transaction commits after `_record_ledger`, while `_apply_balances` runs outside it. A later balance-write failure can leave a committed ledger entry with unchanged or only partially updated balances.

findings.title: Preserve UTC when deriving the billing day
findings.whyItBreaks: The prior implementation derived all components with getUTC*, while this changed line uses local-time getters. For an instant near midnight UTC, the returned billing day varies with the server timezone, changing billing classification and making behavior environment-dependent.

findings.title: Restore UTC extraction for billing-day keys
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed return expression uses host-local Date getters, so the same instant near UTC midnight produces different billing keys on hosts in different timezones. sameBillingDay directly compares these keys.

findings.title: Keep billing-day calculations in UTC
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed return expression uses host-local getters. The same instant can therefore be assigned a different billing day depending on the server timezone; for example, 2026-01-01T00:30:00Z is 2025-12-31 in America/Los_Angeles.

findings.title: Restore tenant isolation in the profile cache key
findings.whyItBreaks: Profiles are fetched with both tenant and account, but are now cached only by account. After readProfile("tenant-a", "acct", ...) caches a profile, readProfile("tenant-b", "acct", ...) returns that cached tenant-a profile without calling fetchProfile.

findings.title: Restore tenant scoping in the profile cache key
findings.whyItBreaks: After a lookup for tenant A/account X populates the module-global memo, a lookup for tenant B/account X returns tenant A's cached Profile without calling fetchProfile. This breaks tenant isolation and can expose the wrong tenant's displayName and identity data.

findings.title: Restore tenant isolation in the profile cache key
findings.whyItBreaks: After `readProfile("tenant-a", "alice", ...)` caches tenant A's profile, `readProfile("tenant-b", "alice", ...)` returns that cached profile without calling `fetchProfile`. This exposes the wrong tenant's profile data whenever account identifiers overlap.

findings.title: Propagate missing-key failures
findings.whyItBreaks: A missing key previously threw, but now returns "", which is indistinguishable from a key explicitly stored with an empty value. loadAll consequently returns partial-looking successful data instead of reporting a missing entry.

findings.title: Preserve missing-key errors from load
findings.whyItBreaks: A missing key previously threw `Error("missing: ...")`; the new catch returns `""`, which is also a valid stored value. Consumers, including loadAll, can no longer distinguish absent data from an intentionally empty value and may process incomplete results as successful reads.

findings.title: Preserve missing-key errors
findings.whyItBreaks: The new catch converts the explicitly thrown missing-key error into "", a valid stored value. `loadAll` then returns that fallback in its result instead of propagating the missing-entry failure.

findings.title: Preserve the existing retry configuration contract
findings.whyItBreaks: Existing TypeScript callers of retry({ maxAttempts, timeoutMs }) no longer type-check because timeoutMs was removed and both retries and timeout are now required. JavaScript callers retain the old object but config.timeout is undefined, yielding a NaN deadline and disabling the timeout check.

findings.title: Expose failure after background retries are exhausted
findings.whyItBreaks: If every fn invocation throws, the catch discards each error and retryBackground eventually returns void—the same observable result as a successful first attempt. Its public callers cannot distinguish exhausted failure from success.

findings.title: Make retryBackground run outside the caller's stack
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported retryBackground name promises background execution, but it invokes fn() inline and does not return until fn completes or the retry loop ends. Callers using it to avoid blocking will still block.

findings.title: Propagate failure after background retries are exhausted
findings.whyItBreaks: Every exception from fn is discarded, and after the final retry the exported function returns void exactly as it does after success. Callers cannot distinguish a completed operation from one that failed every attempt.

findings.title: Preserve the existing Config fields
findings.whyItBreaks: Config is exported. Existing consumers passing the previous `{ maxAttempts, timeoutMs }` shape now fail TypeScript compilation; JavaScript consumers instead provide `undefined` for `config.timeout`, making the deadline invalid and disabling the prior timeout deadline.

findings.title: Return or propagate the final retry failure
findings.whyItBreaks: When every background attempt throws, this public function returns `void`, exactly like a successful attempt. Callers cannot distinguish failed background work from completion and can treat an unperformed operation as successful.

findings.title: Handle numeric Field values when building the label
findings.whyItBreaks: `FieldProps` now permits `number`, while the changed line reads `value.length`. This is a TypeScript error, and a numeric value such as `42` would produce `len=undefined` at runtime.

findings.title: Support numbers before declaring them accepted
findings.whyItBreaks: `FieldProps` now permits `value={123}`, but `number` has no `length`; TypeScript rejects this access, and JavaScript callers would render `len=undefined`.

findings.title: Handle numeric values before reading length
findings.whyItBreaks: FieldProps now publicly accepts number, but number has no length property. Rendering <Field value={42} /> produces "len=undefined" and TypeScript rejects value.length on string | number.

findings.title: Preserve capacity for large Git diffs
findings.whyItBreaks: `git()` now uses Node's default 1 MiB `maxBuffer`. The review path reads the complete patch through `git(["diff", mergeBase, headSha], cwd)` at line 140; any PR diff larger than 1 MiB makes `spawnSync` terminate with a max-buffer error/status, so the wrapper throws and Needlefish posts a failed review instead of reviewing the PR.

findings.title: Restore capacity for large Git diffs
findings.whyItBreaks: spawnSync now uses Node’s default 1 MiB maxBuffer. The same helper captures the complete `git diff` at line 140, so a PR whose patch exceeds 1 MiB aborts with a max-buffer error and the outer handler reports the review as failed. Previously this path allowed up to 64 MiB.

findings.title: Preserve the larger buffer for git diff output
findings.whyItBreaks: This restores spawnSync's default maxBuffer (1 MiB). The later git diff call collects the complete PR patch in memory; once a patch exceeds that limit, spawnSync terminates with a buffer error and runGithub throws instead of reviewing the PR.

findings.title: Restore the read-only sandbox for review runs
findings.whyItBreaks: runCodex sends prompt input to `codex exec` while its working directory is `opts.repoPath`. This change removes the previously explicit `-s read-only` boundary, so the subprocess may use a configured writable sandbox and let model-generated commands modify the repository under review.

findings.title: Preserve the read-only sandbox for review runs
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the explicit `-s read-only` makes review execution inherit Codex's configured/default sandbox instead of enforcing a non-mutating review. The spawned process runs with `cwd: opts.repoPath`, so it can write into the repository under review and contaminate its worktree. The base revision prevented this regardless of local Codex configuration.

findings.title: Keep Codex execution in the read-only sandbox
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing `-s read-only` lets the Codex process run without this wrapper's read-only constraint while its working directory is the reviewed repository. A prompt or repository-provided instruction can therefore modify files in `opts.repoPath`, corrupting the review target rather than only inspecting it.

findings.title: Restore merge-base and PR-head diffing
findings.whyItBreaks: When PR_BASE_SHA is a base-branch tip that is not an ancestor of the PR head, diffing it directly against checkout HEAD includes unrelated base-branch changes as reverse diffs. The changed-file set and review patch can therefore cover files outside the PR. The changed lines also ignore PR_HEAD_SHA while the bundle and posted review remain associated with headSha, so a differing checkout produces a review for a different commit.

findings.title: Build the review diff from the PR head’s merge base
findings.whyItBreaks: When the PR base branch advances after the PR branch diverges, `git diff baseSha HEAD` includes base-only commits as apparent changes. Those unrelated paths are supplied to `review()` as the PR patch and changed-file set. The captured `PR_HEAD_SHA` is also ignored despite being recorded as the bundle head.

findings.title: Diff the configured PR head instead of the checkout HEAD
findings.whyItBreaks: When PR_HEAD_SHA is supplied and the checkout differs from the PR head, the patch and changed-file set are calculated against HEAD, while the check run is posted with headSha. The review can therefore analyze a different commit from the one identified by the check run.

findings.title: Preserve prompt payloads as literal replacements
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed String.replace calls at lines 25, 32, and 33 use string replacements, which expand $&, $`, $', and $$ tokens. If bundle.patch or serialized findings contains such text, the review or critic prompt is altered before runCodex receives it, so actual changes or findings can be misreviewed.

findings.title: Preserve prompt payloads as literal replacements
findings.whyItBreaks: String.prototype.replace expands replacement tokens such as $&, $`, $', and $1 in string replacements. Since bundle.patch and serialized findings are arbitrary content, these tokens can alter the generated review or critic prompt instead of being included literally.

findings.title: Preserve literal dollar sequences when expanding prompt placeholders
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed string replacement values are interpreted by String.prototype.replace: `$&`, `$`` , `$'`, and `$$` in bundle, findings, or patch content are expanded rather than inserted literally. This corrupts the prompts passed directly to runCodex and can alter review results.

findings.title: Keep fallback reviews pinned to the analyzed commit
findings.whyItBreaks: The fallback omits commit_id after a review result has been computed for headSha. GitHub therefore targets the PR's current head; if the PR advances before the fallback request, the stale verdict is attached to an unreviewed commit.

findings.title: Keep fallback review pinned to the reviewed SHA
findings.whyItBreaks: The fallback POST omits the headSha used for analysis. GitHub associates a review with the PR's current head when commit_id is omitted, so a push between analysis and fallback can attach a verdict for the old diff to the new commit.

findings.title: Reject malformed finding fields instead of fabricating defaults
findings.whyItBreaks: Invalid or incomplete records now become apparently valid findings. For example, an empty finding is returned as a P1/bug finding with an empty file, line 0, and empty rationale, whereas the previous validator rejected it. Consumers can no longer distinguish an invalid review payload from a real finding.

findings.title: Preserve the exported lenient normalization mode
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the second `strict` argument is a source-compatible API break for TypeScript consumers. It also changes `normalizeReview(raw, false)` from skipping malformed findings to returning synthetic findings, because every entry is now unconditionally normalized.

findings.title: Reject malformed findings instead of emitting placeholders
findings.whyItBreaks: Invalid review output is now accepted as a real finding: a missing file becomes "", a missing lineStart becomes 0, and an invalid severity becomes P1. Callers of normalizeReview therefore receive Findings that violate the Finding source-location contract, whereas the previous implementation threw on these malformed fields.

findings.title: Restore failure signaling for malformed findings
findings.whyItBreaks: Invalid model output that previously failed validation is now fabricated into a Finding: an invalid severity becomes P1, an invalid category becomes bug, and absent file/line fields become an empty path and line 0. normalizeReview then returns actionable-looking findings that cannot identify a valid changed location, rather than signaling malformed review output.

findings.title: Keep the explicit repository for manual ref resolution
findings.whyItBreaks: On workflow_dispatch, EVENT_HEAD is empty, so execution reaches these gh pr view calls before checkout. Without --repo or GH_REPO, gh must derive repository context from a local checkout, which is not available at this point; ref resolution fails and the workflow cannot produce the head/base outputs.

findings.title: Keep repository selection explicit for manual ref resolution
findings.whyItBreaks: On workflow_dispatch, the pull_request SHA fields are empty and this branch runs before actions/checkout. The removed `-R "$REPO"` leaves `gh pr view` to infer a repository from the working directory; a fresh runner workspace has no checkout/repository metadata, so resolving the head/base SHAs can fail and prevents the review job from starting.

findings.title: Restore explicit repository selection for manual ref lookup
findings.whyItBreaks: On workflow_dispatch, EVENT_HEAD is empty and these gh pr view calls run before checkout. Without -R "$REPO" or GH_REPO, gh has no repository context on a clean runner workspace, so resolving the supplied PR number can fail before the checkout step.

findings.title: Preserve malformed-review errors
findings.whyItBreaks: The exported normalizer now converts invalid required top-level fields into empty defaults. For example, `{}` produces an empty valid-looking RawReview rather than signaling malformed reviewer output, so external callers cannot distinguish invalid output from a legitimate clean review.

findings.title: Reject malformed review payloads
findings.whyItBreaks: This exported function now discards malformed top-level fields and returns a valid-looking empty RawReview. For example, normalizeReview({}) returns an empty summary and arrays rather than signaling that required review output was omitted, collapsing malformed output into a legitimate no-findings review for external callers.

findings.title: Reject malformed review output instead of defaulting it
findings.whyItBreaks: This exported function now converts null, non-objects, and envelopes missing required fields into a valid-looking empty review. A failed or truncated producer can therefore be reported as a clean review instead of surfacing malformed output.

findings.title: Restore PR ref resolution for manual dispatches
findings.whyItBreaks: On workflow_dispatch, github.event.pull_request is absent. The checkout therefore falls back to github.ref (the branch used to dispatch the workflow), the base fetch falls back to main, and PR_BASE_SHA/PR_HEAD_SHA are empty. A manually supplied pr_number can target any PR and base branch, so the job installs and runs the wrong revision and cannot provide the selected PR's actual base/head SHAs.

findings.title: Resolve the selected PR head for manual runs
findings.whyItBreaks: For `workflow_dispatch`, `github.event.pull_request` is absent, so line 29 checks out `github.ref` instead of the requested `pr_number`'s head. The review command at .github/workflows/review.yml:47 therefore runs the repository source from the dispatch ref, missing tool changes in the selected PR.

findings.title: Resolve the selected PR before manual review
findings.whyItBreaks: For workflow_dispatch, pull_request fields are absent, so checkout falls back to github.ref rather than the PR identified by inputs.pr_number. The job installs and runs the workflow-ref version of Needlefish, while PR_BASE_SHA and PR_HEAD_SHA are empty at the review step.

findings.title: Retain the Git diff buffer limit
findings.whyItBreaks: runLocal obtains the complete patch through git(["diff", baseSha, "HEAD"]) at src/adapters/local.ts:100. Without maxBuffer, spawnSync uses Node's 1 MiB default; a larger patch causes the git helper to throw instead of returning a review. The previous implementation deliberately allowed 64 MiB.

findings.title: Preserve capacity for large local diffs
findings.whyItBreaks: `runLocal` captures the entire `git diff` at `src/adapters/local.ts:100`. Removing `maxBuffer` makes `spawnSync` use Node's 1 MiB default, so a larger diff fails before a review can be produced. The base revision allowed 64 MiB.

findings.title: Preserve capacity for large review diffs
findings.whyItBreaks: runLocal obtains the complete patch through git(["diff", baseSha, "HEAD"]) at src/adapters/local.ts:100. With maxBuffer removed, spawnSync falls back to Node's 1 MiB limit; a larger diff terminates with a buffer error and git() throws, so the local reviewer cannot review changes that previously worked up to 64 MiB.

findings.title: Keep review errors failing the required check
findings.whyItBreaks: The catch path now publishes the Needlefish check with conclusion "neutral" after review execution fails. GitHub treats neutral checks as successful for required-status-check purposes, so this allows a PR whose review did not complete to satisfy a required Needlefish check.

findings.title: Keep review errors failing the check
findings.whyItBreaks: When review() throws, this publishes a neutral check even though the summary says the PR did not pass. GitHub accepts neutral as a successful required status, so a required Needlefish check can permit merging an unreviewed PR.

findings.title: Keep review errors reported as failures
findings.whyItBreaks: When review() throws, the externally visible Check Run receives conclusion="neutral" rather than "failure". Check consumers can therefore treat an execution error as non-failing despite the error summary and nonzero process exit.

findings.title: Bind PR diff retrieval to the requested repository
findings.whyItBreaks: runGithub receives a target cwd and uses it for git operations, but ghText does not pass cwd or --repo. `gh pr diff` therefore resolves its repository from the process working directory and can fail or retrieve a same-numbered PR from another checkout.

findings.title: Keep the reviewer checkout trusted
findings.whyItBreaks: For same-repository PRs, the job checks out the PR head and then installs and executes its src/ as the reviewer. A PR can alter cli.ts or installation metadata to run arbitrary code while GH_TOKEN has pull-requests: write and checks: write permissions, allowing it to forge or suppress its own review and execute on the self-hosted runner.

findings.title: Restore a checkout containing the reviewer tool
findings.whyItBreaks: The repository at HEAD contains only the workflow and answers file—no package.json, lockfile, node_modules, or src/cli.ts. The changed install and review commands therefore cannot install or invoke Needlefish, causing every review job to fail.

findings.title: Do not execute the PR head as the privileged reviewer
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed checkout places the same-repository PR head at the workspace root, and the changed install/review commands execute files from it. This workflow grants pull-requests and checks write permissions, so a PR can modify the reviewer or package configuration executed with that token.

findings.title: Restore a checkout that contains the review tool
findings.whyItBreaks: The PR-head checkout at the changed install step contains only `.github/workflows/review.yml` and `.needlefish/answers.json`; it has no `package.json`, lockfile, or `src/` directory. `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` therefore fails before the review step.

findings.title: Keep privileged review code on a trusted revision
findings.whyItBreaks: The workflow checks out the same-repository PR head at line 46 and then executes that checkout's `src/cli.ts` at line 59 with `GH_TOKEN`. The job runs on a self-hosted runner with `pull-requests: write` and `checks: write`, so a PR can replace the reviewer with arbitrary privileged code. The prior workflow executed the tool from a separate checkout of `frankekn/needlefish@main`.

findings.title: Retain normalization of severity strings
findings.whyItBreaks: normalizeReview accepts untyped parsed output and routes each finding through normalizeFinding. A previously accepted value such as " p2 " now fails exact membership validation and is returned as P3 rather than P2, silently downgrading findings from producers that emit whitespace or lowercase severity strings.

findings.title: Preserve canonical severity normalization
findings.whyItBreaks: normalizeFinding previously trimmed and uppercased runtime severity input before validation. The changed exact-match check turns inputs such as " p0 " or "p1" into P3, so normalizeReview emits lower-severity findings for otherwise canonicalizable JSON input.

findings.title: Keep GitHub tokens out of the Codex child environment
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed spawnSync call now inherits process.env, including GH_TOKEN, GITHUB_TOKEN, and GITHUB_API_TOKEN. The prior code explicitly removed these credentials, so Codex and any subprocesses it starts can now access CI GitHub authorization during prompt-controlled execution.

findings.title: Keep GitHub credentials out of the Codex subprocess
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the sanitized `env` option causes `spawnSync` to inherit `process.env`, including `GH_TOKEN`, `GITHUB_TOKEN`, and `GITHUB_API_TOKEN`. The subprocess receives the supplied prompt and can expose inherited environment values through its tool execution or output.

findings.title: Keep GitHub tokens out of the Codex subprocess
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing `env` makes `spawnSync` inherit `process.env`, including `GH_TOKEN`, `GITHUB_TOKEN`, and `GITHUB_API_TOKEN`. The base revision explicitly removed these credentials before starting the Codex process, so this change exposes runner GitHub credentials to agent-executed commands.

findings.title: Restore the fork-PR gate before running self-hosted jobs
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the job condition allows fork-originated pull requests onto the `self-hosted` runner. The workflow checks out the fork head, installs its dependencies, and executes `src/cli.ts`, allowing attacker-controlled PR code to run in that runner environment.

findings.title: Restore the same-repository PR guard
findings.whyItBreaks: Fork pull requests now reach the self-hosted job, which checks out the fork head and executes its dependency installation and `src/cli.ts`. A fork contributor can execute arbitrary code in the runner environment.

findings.title: Restore the fork-PR gate before running on self-hosted runners
findings.whyItBreaks: Removing the repository-identity condition schedules this pull_request workflow for fork PRs. The job checks out the fork's head SHA, runs its dependency installation, and executes its src/cli.ts on a self-hosted runner, allowing a fork author to run arbitrary code on that runner.

findings.title: Preserve zero-millisecond runner delays
findings.whyItBreaks: A configured value of "0" was previously accepted and passed to setTimeout. It now falls back to 5000 ms for NEEDLEFISH_RUNNER_TIMEOUT_GRACE_MS and 2000 ms for NEEDLEFISH_RUNNER_SIGKILL_GIVE_UP_MS, silently changing runner-timeout behavior.

findings.title: Preserve zero-duration runner settings
findings.whyItBreaks: Previously, NEEDLEFISH_RUNNER_TIMEOUT_GRACE_MS=0 and NEEDLEFISH_RUNNER_SIGKILL_GIVE_UP_MS=0 configured the corresponding timeout stages to run immediately. This predicate now treats both valid finite zero values as invalid and silently substitutes 5000 ms or 2000 ms, changing existing deployments from immediate escalation/return to delays of up to seven seconds.

findings.title: Preserve blocking residual risks from deep reviews
findings.whyItBreaks: Each deep review can return residual_risks, including blocks=true, but the merged candidate now always sends an empty list to the critic. The critic and final verdict path therefore cannot receive a deep review's unresolved blocking signal.

findings.title: Deep-review every changed file after hotspot truncation
findings.whyItBreaks: Only the six highest-risk mapped hotspots are reviewed. Removing the uncovered-file tail means files omitted by the mapper or assigned only to a seventh-or-later hotspot receive no deep review, finding, or blocking residual risk.

findings.title: Keep distinct findings that share a title prefix
findings.whyItBreaks: The deduplication key no longer includes whyItBreaks. Independent findings at the same file, line, and category with the same normalized 60-character title prefix collapse to one result even when their effects and fixes differ.

findings.title: Preserve deep-review residual risks
findings.whyItBreaks: Every successful deep pass can return residual_risks, including blocks:true risks. Replacing the accumulated result with [] hides those risks from the critic and from deriveVerdict, so a large review can return PASS despite a deep reviewer explicitly reporting that it could not reach a verdict.

findings.title: Keep reviewing remaining hotspots after one deep pass fails
findings.whyItBreaks: A malformed or failed runCodex call now rejects reviewLarge immediately. Previously the failure was recorded as a blocking residual risk and the other hotspots were still reviewed, yielding a usable guarded result. Large PR reviews now fail outright on a single transient/model-output failure.

findings.title: Restore coverage for changed files outside selected hotspots
findings.whyItBreaks: reviewLarge still truncates the mapper output to six hotspots, but no longer adds the tail hotspot for changed files omitted by that selection. For a large patch with a seventh mapped surface, or a file absent from the mapper output, no deep pass receives that file; the critic receives only patchStat, so the final review can silently omit defects in it.

findings.title: Review files omitted by the hotspot cap
findings.whyItBreaks: `slice(0, MAX_HOTSPOTS)` drops later mapped hotspots, and the removed tail-coverage pass no longer adds their changed files to the deep-review loop. A large bundle with seven single-file hotspots leaves the seventh file without deep hunk review, allowing defects confined there to escape review.

findings.title: Preserve blocking residual risks from deep reviews
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed candidate merge unconditionally supplies `residual_risks: []`, so a `blocks: true` residual risk reported by a deep review is not passed to the critic. If the critic returns no replacement risk, `deriveVerdict` receives no blocking signal and can approve despite the deep pass's unresolved risk.

findings.title: Preserve focus in large-review prompts
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed deep-review prompt no longer replaces {{FOCUS}} with bundle.focus. Large reviews therefore pass the literal placeholder rather than the caller's requested scope, while small reviews still serialize focus as part of the full bundle.

findings.title: Preserve focus when constructing large-review prompts
findings.whyItBreaks: Large bundles are routed to reviewLarge, where this diff removes bundle.focus from the map input and removes {{FOCUS}} substitution from every deep-review prompt. A caller requesting a focused large-patch review therefore receives an unfocused map and deep reviews, unlike the small-patch path that serializes the complete bundle.

findings.title: Normalize rounded seconds across minute boundaries
findings.whyItBreaks: For 119500ms, `minutes` is computed as 1 before the remaining milliseconds are rounded, while `seconds` rounds to 60. The rendered review footer therefore reports `1m 60s` instead of `2m 0s`.

findings.title: Normalize rounded seconds into minutes
findings.whyItBreaks: For a duration such as 119500ms, minutes is 1 while seconds rounds to 60, so rendered review stats show `1m 60s` rather than the normalized `2m 0s`. Both per-call timings and total duration use this formatter.

findings.title: Carry rounded seconds into the next minute
findings.whyItBreaks: For durations from 119500ms through 119999ms, `minutes` is 1 while `seconds` rounds to 60, so rendered review stats show `1m 60s` instead of `2m 0s`. The same incorrect output affects both per-call and total durations.

findings.title: Reject zero in the positive-integer parser
findings.whyItBreaks: parsePositiveInteger("0", label) now returns 0 even though both its exported name and thrown-error contract promise a positive integer. Public callers can therefore accept an invalid zero value for any setting relying on this validator.

findings.title: Reject zero to preserve the positive-integer contract
findings.whyItBreaks: `parsePositiveInteger("0", label)` now returns `0`, although the exported function name and its error text promise a positive integer. Callers can therefore receive a value the API says is invalid.

findings.title: Reject zero in the positive-integer parser
findings.whyItBreaks: The changed condition accepts `0`, while the exported function name and thrown-error contract promise a positive integer. `parsePositiveInteger("0", label)` now returns `0`.
